raw_case:
Challenges in identifying papilledema amid myopic peripapillary atrophy in a patient with idiopathic intracranial hypertension and high myopia: a case report


Background

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) is a disease entity characterized by elevated intracranial pressure, which usually accompanied by papilledema. However, diagnosing papilledema can be challenging in patients with preexisting ocular conditions, such as high myopia.

Case presentation

A 39-year-old woman with a long-standing history of high myopia presented with visual field constriction. Her visual acuity was decreased in both eyes, and she also reported morning headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, and intermittent periocular pain. Although she was diagnosed with IIH based on a cerebrospinal fluid study, there was no evident papilledema, which may have been masked by myopic changes in optic nerve and peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer. After treatment for IIH with acetazolamide, her visual field and visual acuity markedly improved, and her symptoms were relieved.

Conclusions

In highly myopic patients, myopic changes in optic nerve head may obscure papilledema, complicating the diagnosis of IIH. This unusual case highlights the need for detailed history taking and a comprehensive clinical evaluation in patients with high myopia. Clinicians should suspect IIH in the presence of symptoms such as pulsatile tinnitus, morning headache, and visual field constriction, even in the absence of evident papilledema.

Background

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) is a disease entity characterized by elevated intracranial pressure without an identifiable cause and presents predominantly in young obese females [1]. Papilledema, optic disc swelling, and high intracranial pressure are critical diagnostic indicators of IIH [1]. However, diagnosing papilledema can be challenging in patients with preexisting ocular conditions, such as high myopia [1]. High myopia with a tilted optic disc and an elevated nasal disc, posteriorly displaced or blurred, frequently mimics optic disc swelling. Conversely, the identification of papilledema can be complicated when myopia is accompanied by severe peripapillary atrophy. The literature has documented the diagnostic difficulties encountered in such scenarios, emphasizing the need for a careful approach to timely and accurate diagnosis [2]. This case report aimed to shed light on this diagnostic dilemma through a detailed examination of a patient with high myopia and IIH.

Case presentation

A 39-year-old woman with a long-standing history of high myopia presented with visual field constriction, reported by the patient to have been present for 20 years. She had well-controlled diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and chronic migraine. She denied any history of trauma and no significant surgical or social history. She reported that peripheral visual field constriction had begun 20 years ago and had worsened over the past two months. She also reported mild to moderate morning headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, and intermittent periocular pain. Her body mass index was 30.14 kg/m2, and blood pressure was normal. Neurological examination revealed normal cranial nerve function.On ocular examination, the best-corrected visual acuity was 20/30 in the right eye with − 9.0 Diopter Sphere, and − 3.0 Diopter Cylinder at 180º axis, and 20/50 in the left eye with − 10.0 Diopter Sphere, -3.5 Diopter Cylinder at 10 º axis. The intraocular pressure measured by air pulsation was elevated in both eyes: 23 mmHg in the right and 25 mmHg in the left. Visual field examination revealed a constricted visual field in both eyes (Fig. 1a). Fundus photography revealed the characteristic features of high myopia, including a tilted disc and peripapillary atrophy (Fig. 1b). Disc swelling was not identifiable in any eye. The average thickness of the retinal nerve fiber layers in both eyes on optical coherence tomography (Cirrus HD-OCT; Carl Zeiss Meditec, Dublin, CA, USA) was within the normal range (Fig. 1c). Macular structures and electroretinography results were normal.
Laboratory tests revealed normal complete blood counts, electrolyte levels, liver function, kidney function, and serum angiotensin-converting enzyme levels. Antinuclear, antineutrophil cytoplasmic, anticardiolipin, anti-aquaporin 4, and paraneoplastic antibodies were negative.Orbital magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed no significant abnormalities, including intensity changes, compression, or kinking of the optic nerve. Brain MRI and magnetic resonance venography also revealed no structural defects, hydrocephalus, meningeal enhancement, empty sella, or venous sinus thrombosis.A cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) study revealed an elevated opening pressure of 27.2 cmH₂O, confirming increased intracranial pressure (IICP). The CSF cell count and biochemistry were normal, and no malignant cells were detected on cytology.Considering her symptoms and confirmation of elevated CSF opening pressure, treatment with 250 mg of acetazolamide tablets three times daily was initiated for IIH symptom control. After one month, her headache and tinnitus gradually improved. Six months later, her visual field had markedly improved (Fig. 2a), and her visual acuity was 20/25 in both eyes. IOP was 19 mmHg in the right eye and 21 mmHg in the left eye. The tinnitus and periocular pain resolved, and the headache was relieved.


Discussion and conclusions

In our case, we identified the potential of myopic changes to mask papilledema. These myopic changes include peripapillary atrophy and the peripapillary gamma zone. The peripapillary gamma zone, formed without Bruch’s membrane, can alter the optic disc border [3]. Since papilledema diagnosis relies on the appearance of the optic disc margin, fundus evaluation may be obstructed by myopic changes despite the two conditions being histologically independent. Despite nearly normal peripapillary RNFL thickness on OCT and no neuroimaging signs of IICP, the patient’s history of morning headache, pulsatile tinnitus, and progressive visual field constriction led to the suspicion of IIH and the confirmation of elevated intracranial pressure. Following treatment, there was a notable improvement in all symptoms, including peripheral vision constriction, which, according to the patient’s account, had persisted for over 20 years. It is important to note that, even in highly myopic patients showing RNFL thickness within the normal range on pre-treatment OCT, the possibility of swelling should not be excluded.This case highlights the diagnostic complexity of IIH in patients with high myopia. However, although the patient reported symptoms for over 20 years, IIH-related symptoms may have been of shorter duration. This case highlights the need for thorough history-taking while acknowledging potential inaccuracies in patient recollections.The literature also emphasizes the need for clinicians to consider a broader range of symptoms, various imaging techniques, and follow-up examinations for an accurate diagnosis. Sibony et al. highlighted the role of SD-OCT in differentiating between papilledema and “myopic, obliquely inserted disc” (MOID) [4]. Features of MOID on SD-OCT, including nasal elevation, oblique entry of the optic nerve, and peripapillary hyperreflective ovoid mass-like structures that correspond to the nasal halo margin on fundus photography, may provide clues for suspecting myopic changes rather than true papilledema [4]. Issac et al. reported a case of optic disc edema superimposed on atrophy [5]. Funduscopic examination revealed minimal bilateral pallor without evidence of papilledema despite severe hydrocephalus [5]. Although OCT of the peripapillary RNFL showed a normal average thickness at the initial visit, their report also indicates that the typical signs of papilledema may not be distinct when disc edema is superimposed on optic atrophy [5].This case report has certain limitations. The absence of parameters such as central corneal thickness and axial length potentially limits the comprehensive interpretation of the clinical findings. Additionally, follow-up assessments did not include Cirrus HD-OCT, which could have provided valuable insights into changes in the peripapillary RNFL.In conclusion, papilledema may not always be apparent in highly myopic patients despite IICP because optic nerve head changes can obscure typical signs. Our case suggests a comprehensive clinical evaluation to diagnose and treat IIH in patients with concurrent ocular conditions such as high myopia. Clinicians should suspect IIH in highly myopic patients with symptoms such as pulsatile tinnitus, morning headache, and visual field constriction, even in the absence of evident papilledema.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 39-year-old woman with long-standing high myopia.
- **Chief Complaint:** Visual field constriction.
- **History of Present Illness:** Peripheral visual field constriction present for 20 years, worsened in the past two months. Associated with morning headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, and intermittent periocular pain.
- **Past Medical History:** Well-controlled diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and chronic migraine.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Body mass index of 30.14 kg/m², normal blood pressure, and normal cranial nerve function. Ocular examination revealed best-corrected visual acuity reduced to 20/30 (right eye) and 20/50 (left eye) with significant myopic refractive error. Elevated intraocular pressure of 23 mmHg (right) and 25 mmHg (left), and constricted visual fields bilaterally.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Fundus photography showed high myopia features (tilted disc, peripapillary atrophy) but no identifiable disc swelling.
  - Optical coherence tomography (OCT) showed normal peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness.
  - Normal macular structures and electroretinography.
  - MRI and magnetic resonance venography: no abnormalities in brain or optic nerve.
  - Cerebrospinal fluid analysis: elevated opening pressure (27.2 cm H₂O), confirming increased intracranial pressure; normal cellular and biochemical CSF findings.
  - Laboratory tests: normal, including autoimmune serologies and antibody panels.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:** The patient's symptoms of visual field constriction, morning headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, and elevated intracranial pressure suggested idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH). However, the absence of papilledema raised concerns of differential ocular or neurological conditions potentially mimicking or masking IIH.
2. **High Myopia with Optic Nerve Changes (MOID):** Myopic peripapillary atrophy and tilted optic discs can mimic or obscure papilledema. This was a pertinent consideration, but symptoms such as headache, tinnitus, and CSF findings strongly supported IIH as the primary issue.
3. **Other Ocular or Neurological Conditions:** Normal OCT findings, normal visual electrophysiology, and normal neuroimaging ruled out optic neuropathies and other structural causes such as optic neuritis, venous sinus thrombosis, or space-occupying lesions.
4. **Final Key Assessments:** Due to the long-term history of high myopia and absence of visible papilledema, the optic nerve changes were deemed secondary masking factors rather than independent causes. The presence of elevated CSF opening pressure ultimately confirmed IIH, despite challenges in diagnosing papilledema.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) complicated by high myopia-associated peripapillary atrophy, which masked visible signs of papilledema.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at this case. The patient is a 39-year-old woman with high myopia, which is a significant factor because high myopia can lead to various ocular complications. Her main complaint is visual field constriction that's been getting worse over the past two months. She also has morning headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, and periocular pain. These symptoms are all important clues.

First, considering her visual field issues and high myopia, I need to think about conditions that cause peripheral vision loss. Glaucoma comes to mind because it's associated with elevated intraocular pressure (IOP) and can lead to optic nerve damage and visual field defects. Her IOP is 23 and 25 mmHg, which is above the normal range (usually 10-21 mmHg). But wait, the OCT showed normal peripapillary RNFL thickness. In glaucoma, we would expect thinning of the RNFL over time. That's a bit conflicting. Maybe it's early-stage glaucoma where the structural changes aren't yet visible on OCT?

Next, she has elevated intracranial pressure (ICP) confirmed by the CSF opening pressure of 27.2 cm H2O (normal is typically less than 20-25 cm H2O). The combination of headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, and visual field loss could point towards idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), also known as pseudotumor cerebri. However, IIH usually presents with papilledema, but her fundus photos didn't show disc swelling. That's confusing. Could it be a case of IIH without papilledema? But that's less common. Also, she's obese (BMI 30.14), which is a risk factor for IIH.

Her history includes diabetes and hyperlipidemia, which are controlled. Could diabetic retinopathy be causing the visual issues? But the fundus and OCT didn't show signs of that. The electroretinogram was normal, so retinal issues like retinitis pigmentosa are less likely. High myopia itself can cause visual field defects due to staphyloma or other structural changes, but the progression over two months and associated symptoms suggest something more acute.

The MRI and MRV were normal, ruling out structural causes like tumors or venous sinus thrombosis. The CSF analysis was normal except for the elevated pressure, which makes me think again about IIH. But the absence of papilledema is puzzling. Maybe she has IIH without papilledema, but how does that fit with the visual field constriction? Or perhaps there's another explanation for the increased ICP.

Another angle: chronic migraine can cause headaches, but not typically pulsatile tinnitus or elevated ICP. Her elevated IOP might be contributing, but the normal RNFL doesn't align with glaucoma. Wait, in high myopia, the optic disc can appear tilted or have peripapillary atrophy, which might make assessing the optic nerve for swelling or cupping difficult. Maybe the disc changes from myopia are masking signs of glaucoma or papilledema?

Putting it all together: Elevated ICP (from CSF pressure) with symptoms of headaches and tinnitus, normal MRI/MRV, obesity, and elevated IOP. Even without papilledema, IIH could still be considered. Some cases of IIH may present without papilledema, especially in patients with high myopia where structural disc abnormalities might obscure swelling. Alternatively, could this be a combination of IIH and glaucoma? The term "hypertensive optic neuropathy" comes to mind, but I'm not sure. The normal CSF content supports IIH. The visual field constriction could be due to both elevated IOP (glaucoma) and ICP (IIH) affecting the optic nerves. However, the normal RNFL on OCT complicates the glaucoma diagnosis. Alternatively, the increased ICP might be causing optic nerve sheath distension, leading to visual field loss without RNFL thinning yet.

Alternatively, could this be a case of normal-tension glaucoma? But her IOP is elevated. Wait, maybe her IOP is higher than usual due to the increased ICP? The intracranial pressure and IOP relationship can influence the translaminar pressure gradient. If ICP is high, the gradient across the optic nerve head could contribute to optic neuropathy even with normal IOP, but here the IOP is high as well. This might exacerbate the pressure gradient.

Another possibility is that the elevated ICP is leading to secondary optic nerve damage, causing the visual field defects. In IIH, even without papilledema, the chronic elevated ICP could lead to optic nerve compression or ischemia. But why no RNFL thinning on OCT? Maybe the damage is early, and the OCT hasn't detected it yet. Alternatively, the high myopia's structural changes might affect OCT measurements.

Given all these factors, the most likely diagnosis seems to be Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH) even without papilledema, especially given the elevated CSF pressure, associated symptoms, and risk factors like obesity. The visual field constriction and elevated IOP could be secondary to IIH, or there might be coexisting glaucoma. However, the normal RNFL thickness might suggest that the primary issue is IIH affecting the optic nerves in the context of high myopia, leading to the visual field loss. The elevated IOP could be contributing, but the main driver here is the increased ICP. So, the final diagnosis would be IIH.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with progressive visual field constriction, elevated intraocular pressure (IOP), and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (ICP) such as morning headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, and elevated cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) opening pressure (27.2 cm H₂O). These findings align with idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), which typically occurs in obese women of reproductive age. The absence of papilledema on fundus imaging is unusual but may be masked by high myopia-related optic disc changes (tilted disc, peripapillary atrophy).

<step 2> Elevated IOP (23–25 mmHg) and visual field defects suggest glaucoma. However, normal peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness on OCT argues against advanced glaucomatous damage. The structural optic nerve changes from high myopia may complicate glaucoma diagnosis, but the preserved RNFL makes IIH-related optic neuropathy more likely.

<step 3> MRI/MRV and CSF analysis exclude secondary causes of elevated ICP (e.g., tumors, venous sinus thrombosis, infection). Normal autoimmune serologies and CSF biochemistry further support IIH. Obesity (BMI 30.14) is a key risk factor for IIH, reinforcing this diagnosis.

<step 4> The combination of elevated ICP, characteristic symptoms (headaches, pulsatile tinnitus), and visual field loss in the context of high myopathy and normal structural imaging points to IIH. The lack of papilledema may reflect atypical presentation in high myopia or early-stage disease where optic nerve head changes are obscured by peripapillary atrophy.

### Answer:
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with progressive visual field constriction, elevated intraocular pressure (IOP), and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (ICP) such as morning headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, and elevated cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) opening pressure (27.2 cm H₂O). These findings align with idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), which typically occurs in obese women of reproductive age. The absence of papilledema on fundus imaging is unusual but may be masked by high myopia-related optic disc changes (tilted disc, peripapillary atrophy).

Elevated IOP (23–25 mmHg) and visual field defects suggest glaucoma. However, normal peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness on OCT argues against advanced glaucomatous damage. The structural optic nerve changes from high myopia may complicate glaucoma diagnosis, but the preserved RNFL makes IIH-related optic neuropathy more likely.

MRI/MRV and CSF analysis exclude secondary causes of elevated ICP (e.g., tumors, venous sinus thrombosis, infection). Normal autoimmune serologies and CSF biochemistry further support IIH. Obesity (BMI 30.14) is a key risk factor for IIH, reinforcing this diagnosis.

The combination of elevated ICP, characteristic symptoms (headaches, pulsatile tinnitus), and visual field loss in the context of high myopathy and normal structural imaging points to IIH. The lack of papilledema may reflect atypical presentation in high myopia or early-stage disease where optic nerve head changes are obscured by peripapillary atrophy.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> The patient's symptoms (visual field constriction, morning headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, elevated intracranial pressure) suggested idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), but the absence of papilledema raised concerns for differential ocular or neurological conditions.
<Step 2> Myopic peripapillary atrophy and tilted optic discs can mimic or obscure papilledema; however, symptoms like headache, tinnitus, and CSF findings strongly supported IIH.

<Step 3> Normal OCT findings, visual electrophysiology, and neuroimaging ruled out optic neuropathies and other structural causes such as optic neuritis, venous sinus thrombosis, or space-occupying lesions.
<Step 4> Long-term history of high myopia and absence of visible papilledema indicated that optic nerve changes were secondary masking factors rather than independent causes.
<Step 5> Elevated CSF opening pressure confirmed the diagnosis of IIH, despite challenges in diagnosing papilledema.
<Step 6> Final diagnosis: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) complicated by high myopia-associated peripapillary atrophy, which masked visible signs of papilledema.
